text: Immigration policies focus mostly on young to middle-aged working-age people. Exceptions exist in parental and grandparental immigration programs to reunite old immigrants who migrated a long time ago and who in the mean time had children and therefore have their (grand)parents left behind in the home country. Parental and grandparental immigration has been part of immigration policies for more than a century and a half, with elderly immigrants almost by definition not causing any significant adjustment problems to the receiving society: no criminal conduct is expected, there are hardly any family members who they may follow in the future, and there generally will not be significant culture-shock issues. Nonetheless the parental and grandparental immigration links have come under severe pressure over recent decades, mainly on basis of quite poor arguments. A host of measures limiting these family ties, consisting of lower quota levels, increased required levels of sponsor financial resources required, tougher health requirements, as well as more family structure variables introduced as requirements, are only a few examples that illustrate the intent of various countries to greatly diminish old aged family unification rights, and consequently that with hardly any justification behind such a policy adjustment. This post aims at providing more details about how the old age family unification has been restricted by the respective new rules of the various countries over time, focussing on four main categories of destination countries — namely, on the one hand the “traditional settler countries” as Canada and Australia and on the other hand “modern settler countries” such as New Zealand and also including the United States, but not yet countries such as Brazil or Israel, that are still developing as relevant settlement countries. The final aim of the post is to highlight, based on a careful assessment, where in the policy adjustments — mainly during the last four to five decades — good reasons have been found in favour of or against increasing (or limiting) immigration and in how far therefore any policy adjustments relating to parental and grandparental (or other) rights under immigration policies have resulted in more or less fairly or justifiable changes and as such possibly requiring that policy adjustment, which ran into great difficulties to fully carry out such changes with maximum respect towards the (grand)parental as well as the immigrant individual and family members concerned — or should rather be repealed, because lacking in significant numbers of valid argument(s).
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
